title: My grandfather and the broken kettle
summary: When the writer was nine, his grandfather opened a kettle on the kitchen table and changed what the boy understood about repair.
body:
My grandfather could repair anything. I don't mean that lightly. A watch, a chair, a radio, a fan, a small wooden duck whose head fell off in 1994 — there was nothing in our house that he had not, at some point, taken apart on his kitchen table.

I remember the first lesson clearly. I was nine. My mother's electric kettle had stopped working. She unplugged it, said the word 'broken', and put it next to the door for the rubbish. My grandfather, who was visiting, walked over, looked at the kettle, and said: 'Wait one day.'

The next morning, while my mother was at work, he opened the kettle. He laid each piece on a clean cloth on the table. He explained that nothing in the kettle was 'broken' exactly. A small wire had come loose. Another piece had a tiny crack from years of heat. He cleaned everything, twisted the wire back, glued the crack, and put the kettle back together. It worked for another six years.

What stayed with me was not the kettle. It was something in his face while he worked. He treated the kettle as if it had been made by another person. Which, of course, it had. Someone in a factory in 1991 had decided where each piece would go, and my grandfather was reading their work the way you read a letter from someone you've never met.

He told me once that throwing a thing away is easy and almost always lazy. 'Most things are not broken,' he said. 'Most things are tired, or dirty, or one small piece is loose. A repaired object is not the same as a new one. It is better. A new object only knows the factory. A repaired object also knows you.'

When he died, he left me his small wooden box of tools. There is a screwdriver in it that he made himself in 1973, because he could not find one of quite the right size. It has never made me feel modern. It has always made me feel old, in the best way — as if I were standing in a line of people who decided, gently, not to throw something away.

This is not a complaint about modern life. Many things are too cheap or too small to repair. But once a year, I open one thing — a lamp, a watch, a kettle — that I would have thrown away, and I look at it the way he did. Not to save money. To remember what respect looks like.

annotations.meaningVi: cái tua vít, vít vặn ốc

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect for an earlier past action
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thì quá khứ hoàn thành (had + V3) dùng để chỉ một hành động xảy ra TRƯỚC một thời điểm hoặc hành động khác trong quá khứ. Trong bài: 'A small wire had come loose. Another piece had a tiny crack from years of heat.' Những hành động đó (dây bị lỏng, có vết nứt) xảy ra trước khi ông mở cái ấm vào sáng hôm sau.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the past perfect (had + past participle) for an action that happened BEFORE another past action. From the essay: 'A small wire had come loose. Another piece had a tiny crack from years of heat.' The wire and the crack appeared before my grandfather opened the kettle. Form: subject + had + past participle.

blanks.explanation: 'My grandfather could repair anything.' This is the essay's opening claim.
A small wooden duck whose head fell off in 1994 — one of the many things his grandfather fixed.
She unplugged the kettle — disconnected it from the electricity — before deciding it was broken.
She put it next to the door for the rubbish — to be thrown out.
He laid each piece on a clean cloth — a careful, ceremonial gesture.
A small wire had come loose — the actual fault inside the kettle.
He 'glued the crack' — fixed the tiny break in another piece.
Someone in a factory in 1991 had decided where each piece would go.
Throwing a thing away is easy and almost always lazy — the grandfather's central judgement.
A screwdriver that he made himself in 1973 — a small but specific tool the writer inherited.
'It has never made me feel modern.' Using an old hand-made tool feels old, not modern — and the writer means that as praise.
'To remember what respect looks like.' The final image — repair as a form of respect for the people who made the object.

translationVi:
Ông tôi có thể sửa được bất cứ thứ gì. Tôi không nói chuyện này một cách nhẹ tênh. Một cái đồng hồ, một cái ghế, một cái radio, một cái quạt, một con vịt gỗ nhỏ rụng đầu năm 1994 — không có thứ gì trong nhà chúng tôi mà ông chưa từng, vào lúc nào đó, tháo ra trên bàn bếp.

Tôi nhớ rõ bài học đầu tiên. Tôi chín tuổi. Cái ấm điện của mẹ tôi không hoạt động nữa. Mẹ rút phích cắm, nói từ 'hỏng rồi', và đặt nó cạnh cửa để vứt. Ông tôi đang sang chơi, đi tới, nhìn cái ấm, và nói: 'Đợi một ngày.'

Sáng hôm sau, khi mẹ tôi đi làm, ông mở cái ấm ra. Ông đặt từng mảnh trên một miếng vải sạch trên bàn. Ông giải thích rằng không có gì trong cái ấm là 'hỏng' theo đúng nghĩa. Một sợi dây nhỏ đã bị lỏng. Một bộ phận khác đã có một vết nứt nhỏ từ nhiều năm chịu nhiệt. Ông lau sạch mọi thứ, vặn sợi dây lại, gắn vết nứt, và lắp cái ấm trở lại. Nó hoạt động thêm sáu năm nữa.

Cái đọng lại với tôi không phải cái ấm. Mà là điều gì đó trên gương mặt ông trong khi ông làm. Ông đối xử với cái ấm như thể nó đã được làm bởi một người khác. Mà tất nhiên là vậy. Ai đó trong một nhà máy năm 1991 đã quyết định mỗi bộ phận sẽ nằm ở đâu, và ông tôi đang đọc lại công việc của họ theo cách bạn đọc một lá thư từ ai đó bạn chưa từng gặp.

Ông từng nói với tôi rằng vứt một thứ đi thì dễ và gần như luôn là lười biếng. 'Hầu hết mọi thứ đều không hỏng,' ông nói. 'Hầu hết chúng chỉ mệt, hoặc bẩn, hoặc có một bộ phận nhỏ bị lỏng. Một vật đã sửa không giống một vật mới. Nó tốt hơn. Một vật mới chỉ biết nhà máy. Một vật đã sửa thì cũng biết bạn.'

Khi ông mất, ông để lại cho tôi cái hộp gỗ nhỏ đựng đồ nghề. Có một cái tua vít trong đó mà ông tự làm năm 1973, vì ông không tìm được cái nào đúng kích cỡ. Nó chưa bao giờ làm tôi cảm thấy hiện đại. Nó luôn làm tôi cảm thấy cũ kỹ, theo nghĩa tốt nhất — như thể tôi đang đứng trong một hàng người đã quyết định, một cách nhẹ nhàng, không vứt một thứ đi.

Đây không phải là lời than phiền về cuộc sống hiện đại. Nhiều thứ quá rẻ hoặc quá nhỏ để sửa. Nhưng mỗi năm một lần, tôi mở ra một thứ — một cái đèn, một cái đồng hồ, một cái ấm — mà lẽ ra tôi đã vứt đi, và tôi nhìn nó theo cách ông nhìn. Không phải để tiết kiệm tiền. Để nhớ sự tôn trọng trông như thế nào.

questions.prompt: How old was the writer when this first lesson happened?
questions.hint: Look at the opening of paragraph 2.

questions.prompt: What did the writer's mother do with the broken kettle?
options: She took it to a repair shop
She unplugged it, called it 'broken', and put it next to the door for the rubbish
She tried to fix it herself
She gave it to a neighbour
questions.explanation: 'She unplugged it, said the word "broken", and put it next to the door for the rubbish.'

questions.prompt: What did the grandfather actually find wrong inside the kettle?
options: A small wire had come loose, and another piece had a tiny crack from years of heat
The kettle had a deep hole
Someone had broken it on purpose
questions.explanation: 'A small wire had come loose. Another piece had a tiny crack from years of heat.'

questions.prompt: How long did the kettle work after the grandfather's repair?
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 3.

questions.prompt: Why did the grandfather treat the kettle 'as if it had been made by another person'?
options: He had forgotten who made it
Because someone in a factory in 1991 actually did make it — he was reading their work with respect
Because he wanted to copy it
questions.explanation: 'Someone in a factory in 1991 had decided where each piece would go, and my grandfather was reading their work the way you read a letter from someone you've never met.'

questions.prompt: According to the grandfather, why are companies' products so often called 'broken'?
options: Because most things are not broken — they are tired, dirty, or have one small piece loose; companies make them look broken so we will buy another
Because factories deliberately use bad materials
questions.explanation: '"Most things are not broken," he said. "Most things are tired, or dirty, or one small piece is loose."'

questions.prompt: What does the grandfather mean by 'a repaired object also knows you'?
options: After you have worked with it, the object has been shaped by you, not only by the factory — so it is no longer anonymous
It means he liked the object more than the user
It means the object understands the user's name
questions.explanation: 'A new object only knows the factory. A repaired object also knows you.' A repair adds the repairer's hand to the object's history.
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 5.

questions.prompt: What did the grandfather leave the writer when he died?
options: His small wooden box of tools, including a screwdriver he made himself in 1973
questions.explanation: 'He left me his small wooden box of tools. There is a screwdriver in it that he made himself in 1973…'

questions.prompt: Why does the screwdriver make the writer feel 'old, in the best way'?
options: Because it makes him look older
Because it makes him feel he is standing in a long line of people who chose not to throw things away
questions.explanation: 'As if I were standing in a line of people who decided, gently, not to throw something away.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 6.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer still open one thing a year to look at the way his grandfather did?
options: To remember what respect looks like
Because the lamps in his house always break
questions.explanation: 'Not to save money. To remember what respect looks like.'
questions.hint: Look at the very last two sentences.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The grandfather says repair is 'a form of respect' — respect for the maker, the material, and the object's history. Think of one thing in your life today that you could repair instead of replace. What would it actually take, and what would change about how you see the object afterwards?
